name: Automotive Fuse Voltage Drop Model
description: Estimates circuit current by measuring the voltage drop across an in-place automotive fuse. Each fuse behaves as a low-value resistor, so Ohm's law applies directly. This model stores one resistance value per fuse type and amp rating, replacing thousands of lookup table entries.

how_to_use.example: A 10A ATO/ATC fuse shows a 0.5 mV drop. Current = 0.5 / 0.0079 = 63.3 mA.
steps: 1. Identify the fuse type (printed on fuse or fuse box lid) and its amp rating.
2. Set your multimeter to millivolt (mV) DC mode.
3. Touch the probes to both legs of the fuse while it is still installed in the fuse box.
4. Read the voltage drop in millivolts.
5. Look up the resistance (mOhm) for that fuse type and rating from this file.
how_to_use.typical_use_case: Parasitic draw testing with the vehicle off. Probe each fuse to find which circuit is drawing unexpected current without pulling fuses or breaking circuits.

accuracy.note: This is a rough estimation tool, not a precision instrument. Expect +/- 10% accuracy. That is more than sufficient for identifying a circuit drawing current that should not be.
accuracy.sources: Resistance values averaged from two independent sources (CaravanChronicles and Power Probe) where both cover the same fuse type. Single-source data used where only one source is available.
limitations: Fuse resistance varies slightly by manufacturer and age.
Very small voltage drops (under 0.05 mV) are below typical DMM resolution.
Large-rated fuses (30A+) produce tiny drops at low currents, making small draws hard to detect.

ATO/ATC.common_in: Most vehicles from 1980s onward, both interior and underhood fuse boxes

Mini.common_in: Modern vehicles (2000s+), typically in interior fuse boxes

Micro.common_in: Newer vehicles, especially imports and compact fuse boxes

Maxi.common_in: Underhood fuse/relay boxes for high-current circuits (cooling fans, ABS, fuel pump relay)

JCase.common_in: Newer GM, Ford, and Toyota vehicles in underhood and battery junction boxes
